title: Colorectal Cancer
oneLiner: Common adenocarcinoma of colon/rectum arising from adenomatous polyps, presenting with altered bowel habit, bleeding, and anemia; screening saves lives.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man presents to the surgical assessment unit with a 6-week history of progressive constipation, ribbon-like stools, and occasional bright red blood per rectum. He feels that he never completely empties his bowels and has lost 4 kg unintentionally. On examination, there is a palpable mass in the left iliac fossa. Digital rectal examination reveals a hard, irregular lesion 6 cm from the anal verge. Bloods show a microcytic anaemia (Hb 95 g/L, MCV 75). He has never had a colonoscopy.

**Dissecting the clues:**
- **Age 72** → peak incidence >65; age is the strongest risk factor.
- **Progressive constipation** → left-sided colonic or rectal mass causing obstruction (narrowed lumen, solid stool can't pass).
- **Ribbon-like stools** → stool squeezed through a narrowed, rigid segment.
- **Bright red blood per rectum** → bleeding from a low rectal or sigmoid lesion; contrast with melaena (upper GI).
- **Tenesmus (incomplete emptying)** → rectal tumour stimulating the defecation reflex.
- **Weight loss 4 kg** → advanced disease – obstructive symptoms reduce intake, plus tumour-induced cachexia (IL-6, TNFα).
- **Palpable LIF mass** → left colon is more accessible to palpation (especially if thin patient).
- **DRE finding: hard, irregular, fixed** → rectal adenocarcinoma.
- **Microcytic anaemia** → chronic blood loss depletes iron stores before overt bleeding is noticed.

**Atypical presentations that will trick you:**
- **Right-sided colon cancer:** insidious iron-deficiency anaemia, fatigue, vague right iliac fossa discomfort. No overt bleeding or change in bowel habit because stool is liquid and lumen is wide. Often picked up on routine bloods.
- **Acute large bowel obstruction:** abrupt onset of colicky abdominal pain, distension, vomiting (late), constipation. May be first presentation of a constricting left-sided tumour. Beware the closed-loop obstruction with a competent ileocaecal valve – risk of perforation.
- **Cancer on a background of inflammatory bowel disease:** young patient (20–40 years) with long-standing ulcerative colitis or Crohn's. Symptoms may be masked by IBD activity; new refractory symptoms or stricture should raise suspicion.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Colorectal cancer (CRC) is an adenocarcinoma arising from the epithelial lining of the colon or rectum. It typically evolves through the adenoma-carcinoma sequence, where a benign polyp acquires sequential mutations (APC, KRAS, TP53) and transforms over 5–15 years.

**Why it matters:**
- **Second most common cause of cancer death** in the UK (≈16,000 deaths/year).
- **High prevalence:** 1 in 20 people will develop it in their lifetime.
- **Screening works:** removal of adenomas prevents cancer, and early detection (stage I–II) has 5-year survival >90%, versus <15% for stage IV.
- **Common pitfall:** attributing rectal bleeding to haemorrhoids without examination or referral; missing anaemia as a red flag in an older patient.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The adenoma-carcinoma sequence:**  
Normal colonic epithelium → hyperproliferative epithelium → early adenoma (APC loss) → intermediate adenoma (KRAS activation) → late adenoma (SMAD4 loss) → carcinoma (TP53 loss). This progression takes years – hence screening windows exist.

**Where does each sign come from?**
- **Bleeding** → tumour fronds are friable and bleed on contact with faeces. Right-sided lesions cause slow, occult bleeding → iron-deficiency anaemia. Left-sided lesions cause overt, bright red blood.
- **Altered bowel habit** → tumour mass narrows the lumen. Left colon (narrow, formed stool) → constipation or alternating diarrhoea. Right colon (wide, liquid stool) – no change until large.
- **Obstruction** → annular ("napkin-ring") tumour encircles the bowel wall → progressive narrowing. If the ileocaecal valve is competent (closed-loop obstruction), distal colon barotrauma → perforation (caecum most vulnerable, LaPlace's law).
- **Tenesmus** → rectal tumour mimics stool in the ampulla, triggering false urge. Also causes mucus discharge (tumour exudate).
- **Weight loss/cachexia** → combination of reduced oral intake (discomfort, early satiety if obstructed), systemic inflammation (IL-6, TNFα driving muscle wasting), and increased metabolic demand.
- **Palpable mass** → left colon tumours are often felt transabdominally (especially sigmoid); rectal tumours on DRE. Right colon tumours may only be palpable if large and in a thin patient.
- **Anaemia** → chronic blood loss >3 mL/day may not cause obvious melaena, but depletes iron stores over weeks to months. Right-sided cancers are particularly insidious because bleeding is occult.

**Metastatic pattern:**
- Haematogenous: portal vein → liver (most common), then lungs, bone, brain.
- Lymphatic: via mesenteric nodes → para-aortic nodes.
- Peritoneal: especially in right colon and appendiceal tumours (pseudomyxoma peritonei).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause / Risk Factor | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (sporadic)** | No identifiable cause; accounts for ≈75% | Age >65, no family history; most common. |
| **Hereditary syndromes** | Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) – APC gene mutation | Young patient (<40) with hundreds of polyps; family history of colorectal cancer. |
| | Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) – mismatch repair gene mutation (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) | Young patient (<50) with right-sided colon cancer, endometrial cancer in family; tumours show microsatellite instability. |
| **Inflammatory bowel disease** | Ulcerative colitis (especially >8–10 years pancolitis) and Crohn's colitis | Known IBD with new symptoms; dysplasia on surveillance (chronic inflammation → DNA damage). |
| **Lifestyle / Environmental** | Low-fibre, high-fat diet, red/processed meat, obesity, smoking, alcohol | Overweight, heavy smoker, poor diet; but alone never diagnostic. |
| **Precursor lesion** | Adenomatous polyp (tubular, villous, sessile serrated) | History of polyps on previous colonoscopy – need surveillance. |

**Most common cause:** Sporadic (age-related). Always think of it first in a patient >50 with suggestive symptoms.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Gold standard for diagnosis:** Colonoscopy + biopsy of any suspicious lesion.  
- **Why:** Visualises entire colon, allows tissue for histology (confirm adenocarcinoma, assess differentiation, microsatellite instability status).  
- **Pattern:** A single finding of adenocarcinoma on biopsy is diagnostic. However, if colonoscopy is incomplete (obstructing tumour), pick next best.

**Alternative / adjunct imaging:**
- **CT colonography (virtual colonoscopy):** For patients who cannot have full colonoscopy (e.g., colonic obstruction, frailty). If it shows a mass, proceed to biopsy via limited endoscopy.
- **CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast:** Staging tool – look for local invasion, lymphadenopathy, liver metastases. Also useful for occluding tumours to plan surgery.
- **MRI rectum:** Essential for rectal cancer staging – T-stage (depth of invasion), mesorectal fascia involvement, nodal status. Determines need for neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.

**Tumour markers:**
- **CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen):** Not diagnostic (sensitivity/specificity poor). Used for monitoring recurrence after treatment (rising level suggests progression). Baseline pre-op.

**Staging system:** TNM (tumour, node, metastasis) → overall stage I–IV.
- **Stage I:** T1–2 N0 M0 (confined to submucosa/muscularis propria, node negative).
- **Stage II:** T3–4 N0 M0 (through bowel wall, node negative).
- **Stage III:** Any T, N1–2 M0 (node positive).
- **Stage IV:** Any T, any N, M1 (distant metastasis).

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **What confirms diagnosis:** Biopsy showing adenocarcinoma (or cytology if ascites/serous effusion).
- **What rules out:** A normal colonoscopy (with adequate preparation) to caecum essentially excludes colorectal cancer (but not completely – a small flat lesion could be missed; consider repeat if high suspicion).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**A. Immediate (first hour) — if patient presents acutely**
- Is there **obstruction**? (distension, vomiting, absolute constipation, pain). → Insert NG tube for decompression, IV fluids, NBM. Cross-match if surgery likely.
- Is there **perforation**? (peritonism, free gas on erect CXR). → Urgent laparotomy/CT, IV antibiotics (e.g., Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS), fluid resuscitation.
- Is there **massive haemorrhage**? (haemodynamic instability, bright red blood per rectum). → Assess losses, cross-match, urgent endoscopy ± embolisation.

**B. Ward (24–72 h)**
- **Confirm diagnosis & stage:** Arrange colonoscopy + biopsy, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, MRI rectum (if rectal). CEA.
- **Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion:** All new colorectal cancers should be discussed – surgeons, oncologists, radiologists, pathologists.
- **Rectal cancer specific:** If T3/T4 or node-positive, neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy (e.g., long-course: 45–50.4 Gy in 25–28 fractions + capecitabine 825 mg/m² PO BD during radiotherapy). This downsizes the tumour before surgery.
- **Surgical planning:**
  - **Elective colonic cancer:** Segmental colectomy (right, left, sigmoid) with clear margins and lymphadenectomy. Laparoscopic approach if feasible.
  - **Rectal cancer:** Total mesorectal excision (TME) – low anterior resection (if enough distal margin) or abdominoperineal excision (if too low). Need colostomy/ileostomy often.
- **Nutritional support:** If obstructed, consider parenteral nutrition. If not, encourage oral intake with high-calorie supplements.

**C. Long-term**
- **Adjuvant chemotherapy:** For stage III (node-positive) and some high-risk stage II (T4, poorly differentiated, <12 nodes examined, perforation). Regimen: FOLFOX (Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV day 1, Leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV day 1, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m² IV then 2400 mg/m² continuous infusion over 46 hours) every 2 weeks for 12 cycles (6 months). Or CAPOX (Capecitabine 1000 mg/m² PO BD for 14 days + Oxaliplatin 130 mg/m² IV day 1, every 3 weeks × 8 cycles).
- **Surveillance:**
  - CEA every 3 months for 3 years, then every 6 months for 2 years.
  - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 6–12 months for 3 years.
  - Colonoscopy at 1 year, then 3 years, then 5 years (unless further polyps).
- **Palliative (stage IV):** Chemotherapy (FOLFIRI, FOLFOX, targeted agents – anti-EGFR if RAS wild-type, anti-VEGF). Consider liver-limited metastases for resection.

**Common pitfalls – what NOT to do:**
- **Don't operate urgently without full staging (CT) –** a patient with obstruction may have widespread metastases; palliative stenting/colostomy may be more appropriate.
- **Don't give neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy for colonic cancer (except very select cases) –** it is for rectal cancer only (risk of radiation enteritis).
- **Don't forget MSI testing –** patients with Lynch syndrome may benefit from immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) if mismatch repair deficient.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Colorectal Cancer | Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) | Diverticulitis | Haemorrhoids | Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------------------|----------------|--------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Colicky (obstruction) or vague; may be absent | Crampy, relieved by defecation, associated with bloating | Constant, localised LIF (sigmoid) | None, unless thrombosed | Crampy, often in lower abdomen; relieved by defecation (UC) |
| **Bowel habit** | Persistent change: constipation, diarrhoea, or alternating; ribbon stools | Alternating diarrhoea/constipation, worse with stress | Diarrhoea (often small volume, mucus) | Normal | Bloody diarrhoea, urgency, tenesmus (UC); diarrhoea ± fistula (CD) |
| **Bleeding** | Bright red per rectum (left) or occult (right) | None – bleeding suggests another cause | Usually none (if present, think colitis or cancer) | Bright red on toilet paper, after stool | Blood mixed with stool/mucus (UC); less common in CD |
| **Weight loss** | Common (especially advanced) | Rare | Uncommon (if present, think abscess) | None | Common (especially Crohn's – malabsorption, inflammation) |
| **Examination** | Abdominal mass (if palpable), DRE: mass; reduced rectal tone (if advanced) | Normal; mild tenderness possible | LIF tenderness, guarding (if peritonitis); mass if abscess | Anal tags, external/internal haemorrhoids | Abdominal tenderness, perianal fistulae/CD; toxic megacolon (UC) |
| **Sigmoidoscopy** | Luminal mass, ulcerated, friable | Normal or non-specific | Inflamed diverticula, not the sigmoid lumen | Normal | Friable, granular, pseudopolyps (UC); skip lesions, deep ulcers (CD) |
| **Key differentiator** | Biopsy shows adenocarcinoma | Rome IV criteria; no alarm features (weight loss, bleeding, age >50) | CT shows diverticula, pericolic fat stranding, abscess | Visible perianal haemorrhoids on exam | Chronic symptoms, endoscopy + histology show chronic inflammation |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Large bowel obstruction**  
  *Presentation:* Colicky abdominal pain, distension, vomiting (late), absolute constipation. On exam: tympanic distension, high-pitched bowel sounds. Abdominal X-ray: dilated colon proximal to obstruction, cut-off sign.  
  *What to do:* NG tube, IV fluids, CT (confirm site, rule out perforation). Palliative stenting (if metastatic) or emergency surgery (Hartmann's procedure with colostomy).

- **Perforation**  
  *Presentation:* Sudden severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, peritonism. Fever, tachycardia. Erect CXR: free air under diaphragm.  
  *What to do:* Immediate IV antibiotics (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS), emergency laparotomy, resection + washout. High mortality (≈30%).

- **Massive lower GI bleeding**  
  *Presentation:* Haemodynamic instability, bright red blood per rectum, sometimes clots. Often from right-sided or cecal tumours.  
  *What to do:* Resuscitate (IV fluids, cross-match), urgent colonoscopy (if stable) or CT angiography (if unstable) to localise bleeding for embolisation or surgery.

- **Liver metastases**  
  *Presentation:* Often asymptomatic early. Later: right upper quadrant pain, jaundice (if biliary obstruction), ascites, abnormal LFTs (ALP, GGT rise).  
  *What to do:* Image with CT/MRI. If solitary liver met, consider resection (5-year survival ≈40%). If multiple, palliative chemotherapy.

- **Local recurrence (especially rectal)**  
  *Presentation:* Pelvic pain, tenesmus, sacral nerve involvement (sciatica, incontinence). Rising CEA.  
  *What to do:* MRI pelvis, PET-CT if considering re-irradiation or salvage surgery.

**Late-presentation patterns in exam stems:**
- Elderly patient with new-onset jaundice, palpable liver, and iron-deficiency anaemia → likely CRC with liver metastases.
- Young patient (20s–30s) with subacute bowel obstruction and family history → think Lynch syndrome or FAP.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
1. **"CRAP"** for colorectal cancer risk factors (not polite but memorable):  
   **C** – Chronic IBD (UC/Crohn's)  
   **R** – Red meat / processed meat  
   **A** – Age (>50)  
   **P** – Polyps (adenomatous, sessile serrated)  
   *Also add: F – Family history, O – Obesity.*
2. **"RIGHT SIDE"** for features of right-colon cancer (occult bleeding, anaemia):  
   **R** – Right-sided  
   **I** – Insidious  
   **G** – General malaise  
   **H** – Haemoglobin low (microcytic)  
   **T** – Tenderness (vague RIF)  
   **S** – Stool unchanged  
   **I** – Imaging mass  
   **D** – Diagnosis often delayed  

3. **"LEFT TROUBLE"** for left-colon cancer:  
   **L** – LIF mass  
   **E** – Early change in habit  
   **F** – Fresh bleeding  
   **T** – Tenesmus (if rectal)  
   **T** – Tightening (obstruction)  

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
- **Any patient >50 with iron-deficiency anaemia should have colonoscopy** – until proven otherwise (even if they have another potential cause).
- **Rectal bleeding + weight loss + change in bowel habit = cancer until proven otherwise** – never dismiss as "piles."
- **A palpable abdominal mass in the left iliac fossa in an elderly patient is sigmoid carcinoma until proven otherwise** (diverticular mass is more tender and acute).
- **Synchronous cancers (2 primary CRCs) occur in ≈3%** – always inspect the whole colon.
- **Mucinous subtype** (more common in right colon) is associated with poor prognosis and peritoneal metastases.
- **Watch for "mismatch repair deficiency"** – if present, think Lynch syndrome and consider immunotherapy.
- **After treatment, a rising CEA >5 ng/mL (doubling pattern) is recurrence until proven otherwise** – order CT immediately.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Starting iron therapy for microcytic anaemia without investigating the source – this can mask the ongoing bleed and delay diagnosis.
- Using haemorrhoids as a "get-out" for rectal bleeding – always do a DRE and rigid sigmoidoscopy (if available) before dismissing.
- Not checking calcium in metastatic CRC – hypercalcaemia from lytic bone mets (rare) or ectopic PTHrP (even rarer).
- Forgetting to order a CT before an emergency laparotomy for obstruction – you need to know if the patient has liver mets; the operation may be futile.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior / registrar:**
- **New diagnosis of colorectal cancer** – prompt MDT referral.
- **Any sign of acute complications:**
  - **Obstruction** not resolved with NG tube (no flatus, worsening distension, vomiting).
  - **Perforation** (peritonism, free air) -> immediate surgical review.
  - **Massive GI bleed** with tachycardia >100 or systolic BP <90.
- **Post-operative:** Anastomotic leak (tachycardia, fever, peritonism, raised CRP) – early CT is key.
- **Adjuvant chemotherapy:** Febrile neutropenia (temperature >38°C, neutrophil <0.5 ×10⁹/L) – call oncology, start broad-spectrum antibiotics as per local policy (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS).

**When to call ICU:**
- **Sepsis / septic shock** from any cause (e.g., perforation, anastomotic leak) – lactate >2 mmol/L, MAP <65 despite fluids, vasopressors needed.
- **Uncontrolled bleeding** requiring massive transfusion (>4 units in 4 hours).
- **Respiratory failure** from ARDS (e.g., secondary to peritonitis) or pulmonary embolism.
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (or ≥3 in one parameter) – escalate within 60 minutes; NEWS2 ≥7 triggers immediate critical care review.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suggests severe sepsis or tissue hypoperfusion; call ICU.
- **GCS <13** – could be due to sepsis, bleeding, or cerebral metastases (check CT head).
- **Creatinine rising >1.5× baseline** – acute kidney injury from dehydration or nephrotoxic chemotherapy (e.g., Cisplatin if used? Not for CRC but other regimens). For CRC, it's usually hypovolaemia from vomiting/diarrhoea or contrast nephropathy.
- **Oxygen saturation <92% on room air** – consider pulmonary embolism (common after pelvic surgery) or pneumonia.

**Remember:** A junior doctor's job is to recognise the unstable patient and call for help early. Colorectal cancer patients are often elderly with comorbidities – they can deteriorate rapidly from a contained complication.

## Colorectal Cancer
**One-liner:** Common adenocarcinoma of colon/rectum arising from adenomatous polyps, presenting with altered bowel habit, bleeding, and anemia; screening saves lives.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 72-year-old man presents to the surgical assessment unit with a 6-week history of progressive constipation, ribbon-like stools, and occasional bright red blood per rectum. He feels that he never completely empties his bowels and has lost 4 kg unintentionally. On examination, there is a palpable mass in the left iliac fossa. Digital rectal examination reveals a hard, irregular lesion 6 cm from the anal verge. Bloods show a microcytic anaemia (Hb 95 g/L, MCV 75). He has never had a colonoscopy.

**Dissecting the clues:**
- **Age 72** → peak incidence >65; age is the strongest risk factor.
- **Progressive constipation** → left-sided colonic or rectal mass causing obstruction (narrowed lumen, solid stool can't pass).
- **Ribbon-like stools** → stool squeezed through a narrowed, rigid segment.
- **Bright red blood per rectum** → bleeding from a low rectal or sigmoid lesion; contrast with melaena (upper GI).
- **Tenesmus (incomplete emptying)** → rectal tumour stimulating the defecation reflex.
- **Weight loss 4 kg** → advanced disease – obstructive symptoms reduce intake, plus tumour-induced cachexia (IL-6, TNFα).
- **Palpable LIF mass** → left colon is more accessible to palpation (especially if thin patient).
- **DRE finding: hard, irregular, fixed** → rectal adenocarcinoma.
- **Microcytic anaemia** → chronic blood loss depletes iron stores before overt bleeding is noticed.

**Atypical presentations that will trick you:**
- **Right-sided colon cancer:** insidious iron-deficiency anaemia, fatigue, vague right iliac fossa discomfort. No overt bleeding or change in bowel habit because stool is liquid and lumen is wide. Often picked up on routine bloods.
- **Acute large bowel obstruction:** abrupt onset of colicky abdominal pain, distension, vomiting (late), constipation. May be first presentation of a constricting left-sided tumour. Beware the closed-loop obstruction with a competent ileocaecal valve – risk of perforation.
- **Cancer on a background of inflammatory bowel disease:** young patient (20–40 years) with long-standing ulcerative colitis or Crohn's. Symptoms may be masked by IBD activity; new refractory symptoms or stricture should raise suspicion.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Colorectal cancer (CRC) is an adenocarcinoma arising from the epithelial lining of the colon or rectum. It typically evolves through the adenoma-carcinoma sequence, where a benign polyp acquires sequential mutations (APC, KRAS, TP53) and transforms over 5–15 years.

**Why it matters:**
- **Second most common cause of cancer death** in the UK (≈16,000 deaths/year).
- **High prevalence:** 1 in 20 people will develop it in their lifetime.
- **Screening works:** removal of adenomas prevents cancer, and early detection (stage I–II) has 5-year survival >90%, versus <15% for stage IV.
- **Common pitfall:** attributing rectal bleeding to haemorrhoids without examination or referral; missing anaemia as a red flag in an older patient.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The adenoma-carcinoma sequence:**  
Normal colonic epithelium → hyperproliferative epithelium → early adenoma (APC loss) → intermediate adenoma (KRAS activation) → late adenoma (SMAD4 loss) → carcinoma (TP53 loss). This progression takes years – hence screening windows exist.

**Where does each sign come from?**
- **Bleeding** → tumour fronds are friable and bleed on contact with faeces. Right-sided lesions cause slow, occult bleeding → iron-deficiency anaemia. Left-sided lesions cause overt, bright red blood.
- **Altered bowel habit** → tumour mass narrows the lumen. Left colon (narrow, formed stool) → constipation or alternating diarrhoea. Right colon (wide, liquid stool) – no change until large.
- **Obstruction** → annular ("napkin-ring") tumour encircles the bowel wall → progressive narrowing. If the ileocaecal valve is competent (closed-loop obstruction), distal colon barotrauma → perforation (caecum most vulnerable, LaPlace's law).
- **Tenesmus** → rectal tumour mimics stool in the ampulla, triggering false urge. Also causes mucus discharge (tumour exudate).
- **Weight loss/cachexia** → combination of reduced oral intake (discomfort, early satiety if obstructed), systemic inflammation (IL-6, TNFα driving muscle wasting), and increased metabolic demand.
- **Palpable mass** → left colon tumours are often felt transabdominally (especially sigmoid); rectal tumours on DRE. Right colon tumours may only be palpable if large and in a thin patient.
- **Anaemia** → chronic blood loss >3 mL/day may not cause obvious melaena, but depletes iron stores over weeks to months. Right-sided cancers are particularly insidious because bleeding is occult.

**Metastatic pattern:**
- Haematogenous: portal vein → liver (most common), then lungs, bone, brain.
- Lymphatic: via mesenteric nodes → para-aortic nodes.
- Peritoneal: especially in right colon and appendiceal tumours (pseudomyxoma peritonei).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause / Risk Factor | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **Idiopathic (sporadic)** | No identifiable cause; accounts for ≈75% | Age >65, no family history; most common. |
| **Hereditary syndromes** | Familial Adenomatous Polyposis (FAP) – APC gene mutation | Young patient (<40) with hundreds of polyps; family history of colorectal cancer. |
| | Lynch syndrome (HNPCC) – mismatch repair gene mutation (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) | Young patient (<50) with right-sided colon cancer, endometrial cancer in family; tumours show microsatellite instability. |
| **Inflammatory bowel disease** | Ulcerative colitis (especially >8–10 years pancolitis) and Crohn's colitis | Known IBD with new symptoms; dysplasia on surveillance (chronic inflammation → DNA damage). |
| **Lifestyle / Environmental** | Low-fibre, high-fat diet, red/processed meat, obesity, smoking, alcohol | Overweight, heavy smoker, poor diet; but alone never diagnostic. |
| **Precursor lesion** | Adenomatous polyp (tubular, villous, sessile serrated) | History of polyps on previous colonoscopy – need surveillance. |

**Most common cause:** Sporadic (age-related). Always think of it first in a patient >50 with suggestive symptoms.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Gold standard for diagnosis:** Colonoscopy + biopsy of any suspicious lesion.  
- **Why:** Visualises entire colon, allows tissue for histology (confirm adenocarcinoma, assess differentiation, microsatellite instability status).  
- **Pattern:** A single finding of adenocarcinoma on biopsy is diagnostic. However, if colonoscopy is incomplete (obstructing tumour), pick next best.

**Alternative / adjunct imaging:**
- **CT colonography (virtual colonoscopy):** For patients who cannot have full colonoscopy (e.g., colonic obstruction, frailty). If it shows a mass, proceed to biopsy via limited endoscopy.
- **CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast:** Staging tool – look for local invasion, lymphadenopathy, liver metastases. Also useful for occluding tumours to plan surgery.
- **MRI rectum:** Essential for rectal cancer staging – T-stage (depth of invasion), mesorectal fascia involvement, nodal status. Determines need for neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy.

**Tumour markers:**
- **CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen):** Not diagnostic (sensitivity/specificity poor). Used for monitoring recurrence after treatment (rising level suggests progression). Baseline pre-op.

**Staging system:** TNM (tumour, node, metastasis) → overall stage I–IV.
- **Stage I:** T1–2 N0 M0 (confined to submucosa/muscularis propria, node negative).
- **Stage II:** T3–4 N0 M0 (through bowel wall, node negative).
- **Stage III:** Any T, N1–2 M0 (node positive).
- **Stage IV:** Any T, any N, M1 (distant metastasis).

**Pattern-recognition:**
- **What confirms diagnosis:** Biopsy showing adenocarcinoma (or cytology if ascites/serous effusion).
- **What rules out:** A normal colonoscopy (with adequate preparation) to caecum essentially excludes colorectal cancer (but not completely – a small flat lesion could be missed; consider repeat if high suspicion).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**A. Immediate (first hour) — if patient presents acutely**
- Is there **obstruction**? (distension, vomiting, absolute constipation, pain). → Insert NG tube for decompression, IV fluids, NBM. Cross-match if surgery likely.
- Is there **perforation**? (peritonism, free gas on erect CXR). → Urgent laparotomy/CT, IV antibiotics (e.g., Co-amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS), fluid resuscitation.
- Is there **massive haemorrhage**? (haemodynamic instability, bright red blood per rectum). → Assess losses, cross-match, urgent endoscopy ± embolisation.

**B. Ward (24–72 h)**
- **Confirm diagnosis & stage:** Arrange colonoscopy + biopsy, CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, MRI rectum (if rectal). CEA.
- **Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion:** All new colorectal cancers should be discussed – surgeons, oncologists, radiologists, pathologists.
- **Rectal cancer specific:** If T3/T4 or node-positive, neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy (e.g., long-course: 45–50.4 Gy in 25–28 fractions + capecitabine 825 mg/m² PO BD during radiotherapy). This downsizes the tumour before surgery.
- **Surgical planning:**
  - **Elective colonic cancer:** Segmental colectomy (right, left, sigmoid) with clear margins and lymphadenectomy. Laparoscopic approach if feasible.
  - **Rectal cancer:** Total mesorectal excision (TME) – low anterior resection (if enough distal margin) or abdominoperineal excision (if too low). Need colostomy/ileostomy often.
- **Nutritional support:** If obstructed, consider parenteral nutrition. If not, encourage oral intake with high-calorie supplements.

**C. Long-term**
- **Adjuvant chemotherapy:** For stage III (node-positive) and some high-risk stage II (T4, poorly differentiated, <12 nodes examined, perforation). Regimen: FOLFOX (Oxaliplatin 85 mg/m² IV day 1, Leucovorin 400 mg/m² IV day 1, 5-FU bolus 400 mg/m² IV then 2400 mg/m² continuous infusion over 46 hours) every 2 weeks for 12 cycles (6 months). Or CAPOX (Capecitabine 1000 mg/m² PO BD for 14 days + Oxaliplatin 130 mg/m² IV day 1, every 3 weeks × 8 cycles).
- **Surveillance:**
  - CEA every 3 months for 3 years, then every 6 months for 2 years.
  - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 6–12 months for 3 years.
  - Colonoscopy at 1 year, then 3 years, then 5 years (unless further polyps).
- **Palliative (stage IV):** Chemotherapy (FOLFIRI, FOLFOX, targeted agents – anti-EGFR if RAS wild-type, anti-VEGF). Consider liver-limited metastases for resection.

**Common pitfalls – what NOT to do:**
- **Don't operate urgently without full staging (CT) –** a patient with obstruction may have widespread metastases; palliative stenting/colostomy may be more appropriate.
- **Don't give neoadjuvant chemoradiotherapy for colonic cancer (except very select cases) –** it is for rectal cancer only (risk of radiation enteritis).
- **Don't forget MSI testing –** patients with Lynch syndrome may benefit from immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) if mismatch repair deficient.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Colorectal Cancer | Irritable Bowel Syndrome (IBS) | Diverticulitis | Haemorrhoids | Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) |
|---------|-------------------|--------------------------------|----------------|--------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Colicky (obstruction) or vague; may be absent | Crampy, relieved by defecation, associated with bloating | Constant, localised LIF (sigmoid) | None, unless thrombosed | Crampy, often in lower abdomen; relieved by defecation (UC) |
| **Bowel habit** | Persistent change: constipation, diarrhoea, or alternating; ribbon stools | Alternating diarrhoea/constipation, worse with stress | Diarrhoea (often small volume, mucus) | Normal | Bloody diarrhoea, urgency, tenesmus (UC); diarrhoea ± fistula (CD) |
| **Bleeding** | Bright red per rectum (left) or occult (right) | None – bleeding suggests another cause | Usually none (if present, think colitis or cancer) | Bright red on toilet paper, after stool | Blood mixed with stool/mucus (UC); less common in CD |
| **Weight loss** | Common (especially advanced) | Rare | Uncommon (if present, think abscess) | None | Common (especially Crohn's – malabsorption, inflammation) |
| **Examination** | Abdominal mass (if palpable), DRE: mass; reduced rectal tone (if advanced) | Normal; mild tenderness possible | LIF tenderness, guarding (if peritonitis); mass if abscess | Anal tags, external/internal haemorrhoids | Abdominal tenderness, perianal fistulae/CD; toxic megacolon (UC) |
| **Sigmoidoscopy** | Luminal mass, ulcerated, friable | Normal or non-specific | Inflamed diverticula, not the sigmoid lumen | Normal | Friable, granular, pseudopolyps (UC); skip lesions, deep ulcers (CD) |
| **Key differentiator** | Biopsy shows adenocarcinoma | Rome IV criteria; no alarm features (weight loss, bleeding, age >50) | CT shows diverticula, pericolic fat stranding, abscess | Visible perianal haemorrhoids on exam | Chronic symptoms, endoscopy + histology show chronic inflammation |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Large bowel obstruction**  
  *Presentation:* Colicky abdominal pain, distension, vomiting (late), absolute constipation. On exam: tympanic distension, high-pitched bowel sounds. Abdominal X-ray: dilated colon proximal to obstruction, cut-off sign.  
  *What to do:* NG tube, IV fluids, CT (confirm site, rule out perforation). Palliative stenting (if metastatic) or emergency surgery (Hartmann's procedure with colostomy).

- **Perforation**  
  *Presentation:* Sudden severe abdominal pain, board-like rigidity, absent bowel sounds, peritonism. Fever, tachycardia. Erect CXR: free air under diaphragm.  
  *What to do:* Immediate IV antibiotics (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS), emergency laparotomy, resection + washout. High mortality (≈30%).

- **Massive lower GI bleeding**  
  *Presentation:* Haemodynamic instability, bright red blood per rectum, sometimes clots. Often from right-sided or cecal tumours.  
  *What to do:* Resuscitate (IV fluids, cross-match), urgent colonoscopy (if stable) or CT angiography (if unstable) to localise bleeding for embolisation or surgery.

- **Liver metastases**  
  *Presentation:* Often asymptomatic early. Later: right upper quadrant pain, jaundice (if biliary obstruction), ascites, abnormal LFTs (ALP, GGT rise).  
  *What to do:* Image with CT/MRI. If solitary liver met, consider resection (5-year survival ≈40%). If multiple, palliative chemotherapy.

- **Local recurrence (especially rectal)**  
  *Presentation:* Pelvic pain, tenesmus, sacral nerve involvement (sciatica, incontinence). Rising CEA.  
  *What to do:* MRI pelvis, PET-CT if considering re-irradiation or salvage surgery.

**Late-presentation patterns in exam stems:**
- Elderly patient with new-onset jaundice, palpable liver, and iron-deficiency anaemia → likely CRC with liver metastases.
- Young patient (20s–30s) with subacute bowel obstruction and family history → think Lynch syndrome or FAP.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**
1. **"CRAP"** for colorectal cancer risk factors (not polite but memorable):  
   **C** – Chronic IBD (UC/Crohn's)  
   **R** – Red meat / processed meat  
   **A** – Age (>50)  
   **P** – Polyps (adenomatous, sessile serrated)  
   *Also add: F – Family history, O – Obesity.*
2. **"RIGHT SIDE"** for features of right-colon cancer (occult bleeding, anaemia):  
   **R** – Right-sided  
   **I** – Insidious  
   **G** – General malaise  
   **H** – Haemoglobin low (microcytic)  
   **T** – Tenderness (vague RIF)  
   **S** – Stool unchanged  
   **I** – Imaging mass  
   **D** – Diagnosis often delayed  

3. **"LEFT TROUBLE"** for left-colon cancer:  
   **L** – LIF mass  
   **E** – Early change in habit  
   **F** – Fresh bleeding  
   **T** – Tenesmus (if rectal)  
   **T** – Tightening (obstruction)  

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
- **Any patient >50 with iron-deficiency anaemia should have colonoscopy** – until proven otherwise (even if they have another potential cause).
- **Rectal bleeding + weight loss + change in bowel habit = cancer until proven otherwise** – never dismiss as "piles."
- **A palpable abdominal mass in the left iliac fossa in an elderly patient is sigmoid carcinoma until proven otherwise** (diverticular mass is more tender and acute).
- **Synchronous cancers (2 primary CRCs) occur in ≈3%** – always inspect the whole colon.
- **Mucinous subtype** (more common in right colon) is associated with poor prognosis and peritoneal metastases.
- **Watch for "mismatch repair deficiency"** – if present, think Lynch syndrome and consider immunotherapy.
- **After treatment, a rising CEA >5 ng/mL (doubling pattern) is recurrence until proven otherwise** – order CT immediately.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Starting iron therapy for microcytic anaemia without investigating the source – this can mask the ongoing bleed and delay diagnosis.
- Using haemorrhoids as a "get-out" for rectal bleeding – always do a DRE and rigid sigmoidoscopy (if available) before dismissing.
- Not checking calcium in metastatic CRC – hypercalcaemia from lytic bone mets (rare) or ectopic PTHrP (even rarer).
- Forgetting to order a CT before an emergency laparotomy for obstruction – you need to know if the patient has liver mets; the operation may be futile.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior / registrar:**
- **New diagnosis of colorectal cancer** – prompt MDT referral.
- **Any sign of acute complications:**
  - **Obstruction** not resolved with NG tube (no flatus, worsening distension, vomiting).
  - **Perforation** (peritonism, free air) -> immediate surgical review.
  - **Massive GI bleed** with tachycardia >100 or systolic BP <90.
- **Post-operative:** Anastomotic leak (tachycardia, fever, peritonism, raised CRP) – early CT is key.
- **Adjuvant chemotherapy:** Febrile neutropenia (temperature >38°C, neutrophil <0.5 ×10⁹/L) – call oncology, start broad-spectrum antibiotics as per local policy (e.g., Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g IV TDS).

**When to call ICU:**
- **Sepsis / septic shock** from any cause (e.g., perforation, anastomotic leak) – lactate >2 mmol/L, MAP <65 despite fluids, vasopressors needed.
- **Uncontrolled bleeding** requiring massive transfusion (>4 units in 4 hours).
- **Respiratory failure** from ARDS (e.g., secondary to peritonitis) or pulmonary embolism.
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (or ≥3 in one parameter) – escalate within 60 minutes; NEWS2 ≥7 triggers immediate critical care review.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** – suggests severe sepsis or tissue hypoperfusion; call ICU.
- **GCS <13** – could be due to sepsis, bleeding, or cerebral metastases (check CT head).
- **Creatinine rising >1.5× baseline** – acute kidney injury from dehydration or nephrotoxic chemotherapy (e.g., Cisplatin if used? Not for CRC but other regimens). For CRC, it's usually hypovolaemia from vomiting/diarrhoea or contrast nephropathy.
- **Oxygen saturation <92% on room air** – consider pulmonary embolism (common after pelvic surgery) or pneumonia.

**Remember:** A junior doctor's job is to recognise the unstable patient and call for help early. Colorectal cancer patients are often elderly with comorbidities – they can deteriorate rapidly from a contained complication.
